meta.title: SPIKE Lab
meta.description: SPIKE Lab develops clinically grounded signal processing and machine learning methods to improve epilepsy diagnosis, localization, and treatment — bridging neuroscience, engineering, and clinical care.

hero.tagline2: The Epilepsy Research Team at Cleveland Clinic

footer.location: Cleveland Clinic Main Campus - Cleveland, OH, USA

about.title: About
about.body:
**SPIKE Lab** (*Signal Processing for Intelligent Knowledge in Epilepsy*) is a translational research laboratory dedicated to improving the lives of patients with neurological disorders, with a primary focus on **epilepsy**.

We develop clinically grounded **signal processing** and **computational methods** to extract meaningful insights from non-invasive recordings (scalp EEG, MEG) and invasive recordings (intracranial EEG/SEEG). Our focus is on **interpretability**, **robustness**, and **clinical relevance** — to improve the understanding, diagnosis, and treatment of epilepsy, particularly in patients with challenging and **non-lesional disease**.

Our philosophy is that effective algorithms must be **physiologically informed** and **clinically interpretable**, supporting real-world clinical decision-making. We focus on challenging problems such as **non-lesional** and **drug-resistant epilepsy**, aiming to identify actionable biomarkers and network-level insights to guide diagnosis, localization, and personalized treatment.

At the intersection of **neuroscience**, **engineering**, and **clinical care**, we translate advanced signal analysis into tools with direct clinical impact.

items.name: AI and Patient-Specific Models
items.description: We develop machine learning and AI methods, built on robust signal processing pipelines, to support surgical planning, predict seizure risk, and tailor interventions to individual patients. By integrating large-scale EEG, MEG, and stereo-EEG datasets, our approaches provide **interpretable**, **patient-specific** guidance for clinicians.

items.name: Brain Connectivity and Biomarker Discovery
items.description: Using advanced signal processing and network analysis, we map functional and directional brain connectivity, identify critical nodes within epileptic networks, and discover electrophysiological biomarkers. These insights help understand seizure propagation, improve localization, and guide diagnosis and treatment — including in **non-lesional epilepsy**.

items.name: Translational Tools and Multimodal Integration
items.description: We translate our algorithms into practical, **clinician-ready tools** and combine EEG, MEG, and MRI data to create a holistic view of brain function and pathology. By bridging non-invasive and invasive recordings, we enhance diagnostic accuracy, surgical planning, and personalized treatment strategies.

members.name: Balu Krishnan, Ph.D.
members.description: I'm an Associate Staff member and computational researcher at the Cleveland Clinic Epilepsy Center, where I study brain dynamics and epilepsy using advanced **signal processing** and **machine learning** approaches. My research focuses on analyzing electrophysiological data to identify biomarkers of seizure activity, understand brain network connectivity, and improve diagnosis and treatment for patients with neurological disorders. Outside the lab, I enjoy working out, cooking, and spending time playing with my son.

members.name: Himanshu Kumar, Ph.D.
members.description: I'm a Postdoctoral Fellow at Cleveland Clinic working at the intersection of **neuroscience**, **signal processing**, and **machine learning**. My research aims to decode brain dynamics from neural recordings to develop computational biomarkers for epilepsy. In my free time, I enjoy cooking, playing basketball, and traveling.

members.name: Guhan Seshadri, Ph.D.
members.description: I'm a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Neurological Institute, Cleveland Clinic. My research focuses on **large-scale brain network organization** in epilepsy using MEG/EEG to identify abnormal functional communities in interictal recordings and understand how epileptogenic networks persist outside seizures. Outside the lab, I enjoy watching movies, exploring new places, and playing table tennis and pickleball.

members.name: Hanu Skanda Banappa
members.description: I'm a Ph.D. student in Electrical Engineering at Cleveland State University. My research focuses on the cross-junction of **neural signal processing**, **control theory**, and **machine learning** to understand clinically relevant neural dynamics. I'm particularly interested in developing feedback-driven neuromodulation strategies and closed-loop brain-computer interfaces. Outside the lab, I enjoy pick-up sports, dance battles, and trail adventures.

members.name: Adam Halford
members.affiliation: United States Military Academy at West Point

contact.title: Contact
contact.body:
We welcome curious students, collaborators, and clinicians interested in epilepsy research.

Email the **principal investigator** for collaboration ideas, research opportunities, or speaking invitations.
